summary: Critical multi-stage attack detected on vm3-forwarder originating from 192.168.100.99. Attack progression: (1) Brute force credential access against Administrator account with 5 failed logon attempts, (2) User account creation ('hacker'/P@ssw0rd), (3) Privilege escalation via local administrators group modification, (4) Multiple persistence mechanisms established (scheduled task 'backdoor', registry run key modification), (5) Obfuscated PowerShell execution with base64-encoded payload, (6) System reconnaissance via whoami. The attack chain demonstrates sophisticated adversary behavior consistent with APT-style intrusion. NOTE: All malicious activity appears simulated via /usr/bin/logger commands, likely representing security control testing. However, the attack patterns are realistic and warrant full investigation. Two detection rules (DR-007, DR-008) generated significant false positives from normal system operations and require tuning. High confidence in attack classification despite simulation context.

incidents.threat: Multi-Stage Attack Chain: Credential Access, Privilege Escalation, Persistence, and Execution
incidents.mitre_technique: T1110.001 - Password Guessing, T1078.003 - Local Accounts, T1547.001 - Registry Run Keys, T1053.005 - Scheduled Task, T1059.001 - PowerShell
incidents.recommended_action: IMMEDIATE RESPONSE REQUIRED: 1) Isolate vm3-forwarder from network immediately to prevent lateral movement. 2) Block source IP 192.168.100.99 at perimeter and internal firewalls. 3) Force password reset for 'Administrator' account and review all recently created accounts (especially 'hacker' account). 4) Remove unauthorized admin group additions (user 'hacker'). 5) Delete malicious scheduled task 'backdoor' and registry run key modifications. 6) Hunt for PowerShell encoded commands execution artifacts and decode the base64 payload (JABjAGwAaQBlAG4AdA...). 7) Review all authentication logs for successful logons from 192.168.100.99. 8) Perform full forensic disk image capture before remediation. 9) Search for additional persistence mechanisms (services, WMI subscriptions, startup folders). 10) Escalate to Incident Response team for comprehensive investigation.
incidents.fp_reasoning: Multiple attack techniques observed in rapid succession (within seconds) from the same source IP, including failed logons, account creation with weak password (P@ssw0rd), privilege escalation via admin group modification, persistence mechanisms (scheduled tasks, registry run keys), and obfuscated PowerShell execution. The coordinated nature, suspicious account names ('hacker'), and encoding indicate genuine attack activity. All events executed via /usr/bin/logger suggest simulation for testing, but the attack pattern represents real threat behavior that should be investigated.

incidents.recommended_action: 1) Correlate with INC-001 as this appears part of the same attack chain from 192.168.100.99. 2) Review all commands executed around the timeframe (whoami /priv execution). 3) Check if attacker successfully gathered privilege information that enabled subsequent escalation. 4) Audit all administrative actions performed after discovery activity. 5) Review security logs for information disclosure that may have aided attacker reconnaissance.
incidents.fp_reasoning: Discovery activity (whoami /priv) occurred in the same timeframe as privilege escalation and account manipulation from the same source IP. The context indicates post-compromise reconnaissance typical of an attack kill chain. Combined with subsequent privilege escalation attempts, this represents genuine malicious discovery activity.

incidents.recommended_action: 1) Verify with SOC team or system administrators if security testing is authorized on vm3-forwarder. 2) Review the nature of 'repeated service failures' - these appear to be benign system events (gpg operations, update-notifier, PCI bridge assignment failures). 3) If unauthorized, investigate why DR-007 rule triggered on normal system operations. 4) Consider tuning DR-007 detection rule to reduce false positives from legitimate system activity. 5) Document as potential rule calibration issue.
incidents.fp_reasoning: DR-007 triggered on multiple benign system events including: legitimate GPG operations by fwupd (firmware update daemon running as root), systemd service management (update-notifier), and kernel PCI resource assignment messages. These are normal Linux system operations, not service stop attacks. The rule appears misconfigured or overly sensitive. The events from 17:20:53 that overlap with actual attack activity were coincidental and properly classified under other rules (DR-001, DR-006).

incidents.recommended_action: 1) Review DR-008 rule configuration - it appears to be alerting on legitimate cron daemon operations. 2) Validate that observed cron jobs are authorized (debian-sa1 for system activity reporting is standard Debian/Ubuntu functionality). 3) Whitelist expected system cron activities to reduce alert noise. 4) Monitor for actual malicious cron job creation (not present in this batch). 5) No immediate response required unless unauthorized cron jobs are discovered during review.
incidents.fp_reasoning: All DR-008 alerts relate to normal cron daemon operations: standard system startup (@reboot jobs), legitimate scheduled system accounting tasks (debian-sa1), and routine cron service lifecycle management. The 'command -v debian-sa1 > /dev/null && debian-sa1 1 1' is a standard Debian system activity reporting job that runs every 10 minutes. No evidence of malicious cron job creation, modification, or unauthorized scheduled task persistence. This represents baseline system behavior that should be whitelisted.
